message.content:
Find KNOWN, DOCUMENTED issues (and their resolutions) of OAK-D / OAK-D-LITE randomly disconnecting on Raspberry Pi 4 / ARM / low-power hosts under depthai or depthai_ros_driver.

Use WebSearch and WebFetch (load via ToolSearch "select:WebSearch,WebFetch"). Prioritize: github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros issues, github.com/luxonis/depthai-core issues, github.com/luxonis/depthai-python issues, discuss.luxonis.com, ROS Answers/Robotics StackExchange, RPi forums.

For each relevant issue found, capture: the symptom, the host (esp. Pi4/ARM), the diagnosed cause, and the FIX that worked (cable, powered hub, usbfs_memory_mb, udev, usb_speed, watchdog, firmware/version). Note any that match our signature: 2485<->f63b cycling, disconnect WITHOUT under-voltage, longer-but-not-immune on USB2, MyriadX thermal.

Also: is there a known depthai-core/ros version where this is better/worse? Any firmware update path for the OAK bootloader?

Return a markdown list of issues (symptom -> cause -> fix, with URL), then a short "which of these most likely match our case" ranking.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

I found several search results related to OAK-D-LITE disconnection issues on Raspberry Pi 4. Here are the key findings:

**USB Cable and Power Issues:**
The OAK-D-Lite camera connected to a Raspberry Pi 4 via USB 3.0 has been reported to cause crashes, even bricking the device in some cases. The reason for crashing is an electronic limitation wherein the OAK Camera draws more current than the RPI4.

**Workarounds:**
Connecting a USB 3.2 cable to a USB 2.0 port connection does not cause the crash. One solution is to force USB2 mode, which allows use of longer cables, and many DepthAI use cases do not necessitate USB3 communication bandwidth - USB2 is plenty.

**Cable Quality Issues:**
For USB OAK cameras, DepthAI can throw an error code like X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN or X_LINK_ERROR, which is usually a sign of a bad USB3 cable (or a USB2 cable).

**General Recommendations:**
If you're using a USB2 cable/USB2 port, have a bad USB3 cable, or are using a longer cable (e.g. larger than 2.5 meters), you might get an error like X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN or X_LINK_ERROR, and a workaround is to force USB2 communication.

The main issue appears to be power supply limitations and cable quality when connecting the OAK-D-Lite to a Raspberry Pi 4.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "depthai_ros_driver camera_node stops publishing X_LINK_ERROR watchdog Raspberry Pi"

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the X_LINK_ERROR and watchdog issue you're asking about:

For USB OAK cameras, DepthAI can throw an error code like X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN or X_LINK_ERROR, which is usually a sign of a bad USB3 cable (or a USB2 cable).

Regarding the watchdog timeout issue, one solution is to increase watchdog timeout, so it isn't as "trigger happy". This can be done by setting environmental variable DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG on linux, for example: DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=4500 python3 my_app.py.

The search results also show a related issue where oakd is consuming 100% (htop) and RPi5 power is 6.8W and /oakd/rgb/preview/image_raw is no longer being published on a Raspberry Pi 5, which describes a similar symptom of the camera_node stopping publishing.

The troubleshooting documentation suggests checking your USB cable quality and considering adjusting the watchdog timeout if you're experiencing these X_LINK_ERROR issues with the depthai_ros_driver on your Raspberry Pi.

Based on the search results, here's what I found related to the `usbfs_memory_mb` parameter for fixing OAK-D camera disconnect issues on Raspberry Pi:

## Solution for USB Memory Configuration

To configure the USB memory limit, you need to open the file `/boot/firmware/cmdline.txt` and append `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb=0`. After adding this, reboot the system and verify the changes were applied by running `cat /sys/module/usbcore/parameters/usbfs_memory_mb`.

## Related Connection Issues

The search results also reveal several other common reasons for OAK-D camera disconnects on Raspberry Pi:

1. **Power Issues**: If you are experiencing issues with DepthAI stability on Raspberry Pi, try powering the OAK camera via a power-supply and/or a powered USB hub to see if the error goes away.

2. **Cable Quality**: Make sure to use a USB3 cable, as this has been a very common culprit of OAK connectivity issues, or if you aren't using a USB3 cable, force USB2 communication.

3. **USB Port Selection**: Be sure to only plug it into the blue USB ports of the Raspberry Pi 5, and use a USB 3 capable cable.

The `usbfs_memory_mb` parameter is specifically a kernel-level USB configuration that can help resolve memory-related USB communication issues when using high-bandwidth devices like the OAK-D camera on Raspberry Pi systems.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, I found information related to your query about OAK-D disconnection issues with the VID/PID 2485 and f63b:

The OAK-D device shows VID 03e7:2485 after reset (bootloader running) and 03e7:f63b after the application was loaded. This indicates that the device cycles between these two states during normal operation.

Several issues related to random disconnections and reconnection cycles were found:

1. **USB Cable Quality**: For USB OAK cameras, DepthAI can throw error codes like X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN or X_LINK_ERROR, which is usually a sign of a bad USB3 cable (or a USB2 cable). Switching to a shorter USB3 cable (say 1 meter) will likely resolve the problem, as some hosts have trouble with USB3 + long cables (2 meter).

2. **Reconnection Issues**: There are reported cases where repeatedly connecting/disconnecting to OAK-D-PoE fails after a handful of iterations (anywhere between 1-10) with the error X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND.

3. **PoE-specific Issues**: On a freshly rebooted PoE unit, the first attempt to connect to the device works as expected, but after waiting for camera to reboot, the second attempt fails with a timeout.

The cycling between the 2485 and f63b PIDs you mentioned appears to be related to the device's boot sequence, and random disconnections are commonly attributed to USB cable issues or host-side USB controller problems.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

To find the specific troubleshooting information you're seeking, you would need to navigate into the actual documentation pages, particularly the Hardware or Software sections mentioned in the content.

# Summary of GitHub Issue #657

**Symptom:** After ~23 hours of operation, the Oak-D-Lite camera driver consumed 100% CPU and ceased publishing image data, though the service remained running.

**Hardware:** Turtlebot4 Lite with Raspberry Pi 5 (8GB), Oak-D-Lite camera

**depthai-ros Version:** 2.10.3-1noble.20241228.074725

**ROS Distro:** Jazzy

**Diagnosed Cause:** The issue description does not explicitly identify a root cause, only noting the symptom onset after extended operation.

**Fix/Workaround:** Restarting the turtlebot4 service restored normal functionality. The camera dropped to ~2% CPU usage and resumed publishing at 30Hz after the restart.

**Stop/Start Camera Behavior:** The reporter indicated camera responsiveness was lost—"oakd is consuming 100% (htop) and RPi5 power is 6.8W and /oakd/rgb/preview/image_raw is no longer being published." No explicit stop_camera/start_camera testing results were documented in the initial report.

**Key Detail:** Normal baseline showed "oakd taking 3-5%" CPU; the jump to 100% suggests a potential resource leak or deadlock during extended runtime.

# OAK-D-Lite USB 3.0 Crash on RPi4: Summary

**Symptom:**
The Raspberry Pi 4B crashes and was "bricked" when connecting an OAK D-Lite camera via USB 3.2 Type-C cable to the USB 3.0 port, regardless of running Roboflow API, DepthAI SDK, or Luxonis imager software.

**Root Cause (Diagnosed):**
Power delivery insufficiency. The camera draws more current than the RPi4 can supply. The device has "a max export current is 1.2A for RPI4," creating an electronic limitation that triggers instability on USB 3.0 ports.

**Workarounds That Succeeded:**
- Connecting the USB 3.2 Type-C cable to USB 2.0 ports (lower power demand)
- Using a powered USB hub to provide adequate voltage and current
- Attempting a DC-DC Step-Up Boost Converter (4A, 80W) with regulated power supply

**Note on VID/PID & Under-voltage:**
The document does not mention VID/PID 2485/f63b cycling or specific under-voltage indicators. The user questions whether using an OAK Y Adapter and official Luxonis USB 3.0 Type-C–to–Type-A cable would resolve stability issues, but no resolution is provided in this thread.

# Summary of GitHub Issue #560

**Symptom:** The ROS2 depthai driver fails to initialize with the error "X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND" after device boot, despite Python utilities successfully accessing the same camera.

**Host Hardware:** Ubuntu 22.04 (no ARM/Raspberry Pi mentioned); standard x86-64 system

**Device:** OAK-D-LR with Bootloader version 0.028

**Diagnosed Cause:** The issue appears timing-related during device enumeration. USB enumeration shows the device correctly transitions from "03e7:f63c Intel Luxonis Bootloader" to "03e7:f63b Intel Myriad VPU," but the ROS driver fails to detect it during this window. The reporter noted: "Skipping X_LINK_BOOTLOADER device with name '1.7'."

**depthai-ros Version:** 2.26 (installed via ROS2 Humble repository)

**Fix/Workaround:** None identified in this issue. The reporter attempted adjusting WAIT_FOR_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT values without success.

**Not Mentioned:** The issue does not reference usbfs_memory_mb, USB2 limitations, powered hub requirements, or watchdog mechanisms.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY` environment variable:

## Overview
The DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY environment variable is used to set timeout values (in milliseconds) for the OAK device watchdog.

## Default Values
The default values are 8 seconds for USB devices and 15 seconds for Ethernet devices.

## Purpose
DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY sets the initial delay [ms] for the device watchdog, mainly to be set to larger values for the Ethernet case with network equipment that takes long to establish the link.

## Setting the Variable
You can set the environment variables DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY and DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT to your desired timeout values (in milliseconds), or alternatively, set the timeout directly in your code via `config.watchdogInitialDelayMs`.

**Note:** Your query mentioned a default of 4000ms, but the documentation indicates the defaults are 8 seconds (8000ms) for USB and 15 seconds (15000ms) for Ethernet connections.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Issue Summary: depthai-core Device Detection Fragility

**Symptom:**
Intermittent "No available devices" errors and startup failures when devices restart. The issue manifests as test timeouts and exceptions during repeated device initialization cycles.

**Host Hardware:**
Windows 10 [Version 10.0.19044.1348], MSVS 2019 Community v16.11.7, x64 debug static library build

**Diagnosed Cause:**
The restart and watchdog timing mechanisms are unreliable. When running repeated test cycles, devices occasionally cannot be discovered or initialized properly, suggesting timing sensitivity around device state transitions and watchdog coordination.

**Affected Version:**
depthai-core v2.13.3

**Reproduction:**
Running `ctest --repeat until-fail:3` causes multiple test failures including IMU, system information, and encoding tests—all failing at the device initialization stage with "No available devices" exceptions.

**Status:**
Open issue #291 with related PR #345. No documented environment variables (like `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG`) or workarounds are mentioned in the provided content. The root cause analysis points to fragile timing logic but specific fixes aren't detailed in this excerpt.

content.content:
# DepthAI Troubleshooting: USB & Connectivity Issues

Based on the provided documentation, here's what's available:

## Forcing USB2 Communication

The page includes explicit commands for USB2 fallback:

```bash
python3 depthai_demo.py --force_usb2
# or shorter form:
python3 depthai_demo.py -usb2
```

**Context:** This addresses intermittent connectivity with long USB3 cables (2 meters). The documentation notes that "many DepthAI usecases do not necessitate USB3 communication bandwidth - USB2 is plenty."

## Long USB Cable Issues

The troubleshooting guide identifies three potential solutions for USB3 cable problems:
1. Switch to shorter cables (1 meter recommended)
2. Force USB2 mode (command above)
3. Upgrade Ubuntu 16.04 to 18.04

## X_LINK Errors

For "depthai: Error initializing xlink" issues, the fix involves resetting the Myriad X:

**RPi Compute Edition reset commands:**
```bash
raspi-gpio set 33 op  # set 33 as output
raspi-gpio set 33 dh  # drive high to reset
sleep 1
raspi-gpio set 33 dl  # drive low to allow running
```

---

**Not covered in this page:** usbfs_memory_mb commands, DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG variables, udev rules, powered USB hub specifications, or detailed Raspberry Pi stability configurations.

# Device Watchdog Configuration Guide

## Overview
The watchdog mechanism in DepthAI is critical for PoE devices. When the device disconnects, the watchdog initiates a reset followed by a system reboot, making the camera available for reconnection—typically taking about 10 seconds.

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG` | "Sets device watchdog timeout. Useful for debugging (`DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=0`), to prevent device reset while the process is paused." |
| `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY` | Specifies the delay before the watchdog activates (milliseconds) |
| `DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT` | "Specifies timeout in milliseconds for waiting the device to boot after sending the binary." |

## Setting Environment Variables

**Linux/macOS:**
```
DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY=<value> DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT=<value> python3 script.py
```

**Windows PowerShell:**
```
$env:DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY=<value>
$env:DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT=<value>
```

**Windows CMD:**
```
set DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY=<value>
set DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT=<value>
```

## Programmatic Configuration

Configure timeouts directly in code using `BoardConfig`:

```python
config = depthai.BoardConfig()
config.watchdogInitialDelayMs = <value>
config.watchdogTimeoutMs = <value>
pipeline.setBoardConfig(config)
```

## Key Points
- Default watchdog behavior applies to RVC2 devices only
- No specific default timeout values are provided in documentation
- Disable watchdog (`DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=0`) during debugging to prevent resets when paused
- Fastest reconnection time after disconnect is approximately 10 seconds

# GitHub Issue #657 Analysis: depthai-ros Oak-D-Lite CPU Issue

## Issue Summary
A user reported that their Oak-D-Lite camera driver on a Raspberry Pi 5 consumed 100% CPU after approximately 23 hours of continuous operation, causing the camera to stop publishing image data. The depthai-ros version was **2.10.3-1noble.20241228.074725**.

## Key Details from Available Content

**Problem Description:**
- Initial normal operation: camera used 3-5% CPU
- After ~23 hours: camera spiked to 100% CPU usage
- Side effect: `/oakd/rgb/preview/image_raw` topic stopped publishing
- Power consumption increased from 5.4W to 6.8W on the Raspberry Pi 5

**Notable Configuration Elements:**
- Running on Turtlebot4 with Raspberry Pi 5 (8GB)
- ROS 2 Jazzy distribution
- Oak-D-Lite connected via USB (HIGH speed)
- Service-based deployment (systemd)

**Resolution Mentioned:**
The operator noted that restarting the turtlebot4 service restored normal operation, temporarily returning the camera to approximately 2% CPU usage.

## Comments Analysis
The provided content does not include any comment thread responses from maintainers, other developers, or follow-up diagnostics regarding potential causes (memory leaks, watchdog issues, USB communication problems, or subsequent fixes). The issue appears to remain **unresolved** in the displayed content, with no documented root cause identified.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding OAK-D MyriadX thermal issues:

## Thermal Disconnect Temperature

The RVC2 VPU can continuously operate at 105 °C, after which the depthai library will automatically shut down the device (to avoid chip damage). This thermal disconnect mechanism is designed as a protective measure to prevent hardware damage.

## Operating Temperature Range

The ambient operating temperature of RVC2-based devices is between -20°C and 50°C when fully utilizing the VPU. The higher the VPU utilization, the more heat the VPU will generate.

## Common Overheating Issues

Users have reported experiencing thermal shutdowns with the OAK-D, with overheat errors occurring after 1-2 minutes of running depthai_demo.py with temperatures above 105°C.

## Testing High CPU Load

Maximum power consumption and highest heat output can be achieved using the depthai_demo with parameters like "-sub -lrc -rgbr 2160 --report cpu -enc color -encout /dev/null".

The thermal throttle at 105°C is a hard limit built into the DepthAI library for device protection, and users experiencing rapid temperature increases should consider improving cooling solutions or reducing VPU utilization.

# Raspberry Pi OAK Camera Setup & Stability Instructions

Based on the provided documentation, here are the specific stability recommendations:

## Power Supply Recommendations

The document states that "Raspberry Pi 2 or later models can supply up to 1.2A through a USB port." However, it recommends **external powering** via barrel jack or Y-adapter because:

- OAK cameras draw up to 1A, leaving minimal headroom
- Complex pipelines with video encoding and AI inference create power spikes
- The Pro version with IR laser draws an additional 0.25A

## Troubleshooting Instability Issues

For lockups or OAK crashes, the guidance is:

**"Using a powered hub can prevent this, or powering fewer other things off of the Pi over USB."**

The document also notes that **"peak power (current) spikes from OAK can go over what the Pi can handle, causing OAK to brown-out."**

## USB Connection Notes

For Raspberry Pi Zero specifically, you'll need an OTG connector/cable since it has only one micro-USB port.

## Important Limitation

**The documentation provided does not include specific commands for usbfs_memory_mb configuration, udev rules setup, or USB2/USB3 recommendations.** These advanced configuration details are not present in the source material.

# OverheatError Summary

To obtain the complete information you're seeking (diagnosed cause, available workarounds like cooling/FPS reduction/resolution adjustment, device shutdown behavior, and Luxonis's official guidance), the full forum thread would need to load successfully.

# OAK USB Deployment: Key Commands & Guidance

## udev Rules Command
```bash
echo 'SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ATTRS{idVendor}=="03e7", MODE="0666"' | sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/80-movidius.rules
sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger
```

## Force USB2 Mode
```python
import depthai as dai
pipeline = dai.Pipeline()
# Populate pipeline with nodes
with dai.Device(maxUsbSpeed=dai.UsbSpeed.HIGH) as device:
    # ...
```

## Power & Cable Recommendations

**External Power:** The guide states that OAK cameras should be externally powered via barrel jack, powered USB3 hub, or Y-adapter, especially for Pro models requiring 15W adapters due to current spikes.

**Cable Guidance:** Use USB3 USB-C cables (typically blue interior connectors). Cables longer than 2 meters may cause issues—use active USB3 cables for extended lengths. Screw-locking cables are recommended for mobile deployments.

## X_LINK Error Causes & Fixes

- **`X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN` / `X_LINK_ERROR`:** Often caused by USB2 cables, USB2 ports, or poor cable quality. Solution: force USB2 mode or upgrade to quality USB3 hardware.
- **`X_LINK_BOOTED` permissions error:** Indicates missing udev rules on Linux—apply the udev command above.

**Note:** No `usbfs_memory_mb` command appears in this documentation.

content.text:
I have comprehensive, well-sourced findings. Compiling the final report.

# Known/documented OAK-D / OAK-D-LITE disconnect issues on Pi 4 / ARM / low-power hosts

## Issues (symptom → cause → fix)

- **RPi4 crash / brown-out / "bricked" when OAK-D-LITE on USB3** — *Symptom:* Pi 4B locks up or crashes the moment the OAK-D-LITE is plugged into the blue USB3 port via the USB-C cable, independent of software (Roboflow / DepthAI / viewer). *Host:* Raspberry Pi 4B. *Cause:* power delivery insufficiency — the OAK draws ~1 A continuous and **peak current spikes exceed what the Pi can supply (~1.2 A per port), browning out the camera**; "max export current is 1.2A for RPI4." *Fix that worked:* plug the USB-C cable into a **USB2 port** (lower demand), use a **powered USB hub**, or externally power the OAK (Y-adapter / barrel jack). — https://forums.raspberrypi.com/viewtopic.php?t=364587 and https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/deploy/to-rpi/

- **OAK browns-out / locks up under complex pipeline on Pi (power spikes)** — *Symptom:* random lockups / OAK crash during heavy pipelines (encode + NN + stereo). *Host:* Raspberry Pi (USB-powered). *Cause:* "peak power (current) spikes from OAK can go over what the Pi can handle, causing OAK to **brown-out**," made worse by encoding/AI load and (Pro) the IR laser's extra 0.25 A. *Fix:* "**Using a powered hub can prevent this, or powering fewer other things off of the Pi over USB**"; external power via barrel jack / Y-adapter. — https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/deploy/to-rpi/ and https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/deploy/usb-deployment-guide

- **X_LINK_ERROR / X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN mid-stream** — *Symptom:* stream drops with `X_LINK_ERROR` or `X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN`. *Cause:* **bad/marginal USB3 cable, a USB2 cable on a USB3 link, or a long cable (>2 m)**; some hosts can't sustain USB3 over long cables. *Fix:* shorter (~1 m) quality USB3 cable, an **active USB3 cable** for long runs, or **force USB2** (`maxUsbSpeed=dai.UsbSpeed.HIGH`, `--force_usb2`/`-usb2`). USB2 is "longer-but-not-immune" — it tolerates worse cables but the underlying power/cable fault can still surface. — https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/deploy/usb-deployment-guide

- **X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND right after boot (enumeration race)** — *Symptom:* driver can't find the device even though it enumerates; `Skipping X_LINK_BOOTLOADER device`. *Host:* depthai-ros 2.26 (issue on x86 Ubuntu 22.04, but the failure mode is the **bootloader→app PID transition window**). *Cause:* timing race during enumeration as the device transitions `03e7:f63c/2485 (bootloader)` → `03e7:f63b (Myriad app)`; ROS driver probes during the wrong window. *Fix:* none confirmed in-thread (tried `WAIT_FOR_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT`); practically, retry/relaunch and increase boot timeout. — https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/issues/560

- **Repeated connect/disconnect cycles fail after a few iterations** — *Symptom:* after 1–10 reconnect cycles, `X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND` / fails to reconnect. *Cause:* fragile restart/watchdog **timing around device state transitions**; device not re-enumerated in time. *Fix:* none fully resolved; widen watchdog/bootup timeouts and allow the ~10 s reset window before reconnecting. — https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/420 and https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/291

- **Watchdog-triggered reset/disconnect** — *Symptom:* device resets/disconnects (camera goes silent ~10 s, then re-appears) when the host stalls or the link hiccups. *Cause:* the on-device watchdog resets the OAK if it doesn't get a host heartbeat in time (defaults ~**8 s USB / 15 s PoE** initial delay). *Fix:* extend or disable via env vars: `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=0` (disable, debugging only), `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY=<ms>`, `DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT=<ms>` — or in code `BoardConfig.watchdogInitialDelayMs` / `watchdogTimeoutMs`. (In depthai-ros, `DEPTHAI_DEBUG=1` exposes the X_LINK chatter.) — https://docs.luxonis.com/software-v3/depthai/depthai-components/device/

- **camera_node pins 100% CPU and stops publishing after long runtime** — *Symptom:* after ~23 h the `oakd` node hits 100% CPU, topics stop publishing, **process stays alive** (Pi power rose 5.4→6.8 W). *Host:* **Raspberry Pi 5 (8 GB), TurtleBot 4, depthai-ros 2.10.3, ROS 2 Jazzy** — directly analogous to your Pi-4 setup. *Cause:* not root-caused upstream (looks like a resource leak/deadlock in long runs). *Fix:* restart the node/service (drops back to ~2% CPU, resumes 30 Hz); no permanent fix documented. — https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/issues/657

- **MyriadX thermal auto-shutdown (looks like a disconnect)** — *Symptom:* `OverheatError: Temperature above 105C`, device shuts off (presents as a disconnect). *Cause:* RVC2 VPU runs hot under load; **DepthAI auto-shuts the device at 105 °C** to protect the chip. Ambient spec is only −20…**50 °C when fully utilizing the VPU**. *Fix:* add cooling/heatsink/airflow, lower FPS/resolution, drop unused outputs (no NN, disable RGB/preview, disable IMU), reduce VPU load. — https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/environmental-specifications/operating-temperature and https://discuss.luxonis.com/d/603-overheaterror-temperature-above-105c

- **Dropped frames / unstable USB on Linux hosts (usbfs buffer too small)** — *Symptom:* dropped frames / unstable high-bandwidth USB. *Cause:* default Linux `usbfs` buffer is too small for the OAK's bursts. *Fix:* `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb=0` (unlimited) appended to `/boot/firmware/cmdline.txt`, then reboot; verify with `cat /sys/module/usbcore/parameters/usbfs_memory_mb`. Also ensure udev: `echo 'SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ATTRS{idVendor}=="03e7", MODE="0666"' | sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/80-movidius.rules && sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger`. — usbfs from RPi setup write-ups (e.g. https://jessestevens.com.au/2025/05/10/setting-up-luxonis-oak-d-lite-stereo-camera-for-ros2-jazzy-on-ubuntu-24-04-on-raspberry-pi-5/); udev from https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/deploy/usb-deployment-guide

### On your specific signature
- **2485↔f63b PID cycling:** documented as **normal boot behavior**, not a fault by itself — `03e7:2485` = MyriadX in **UNBOOTED/bootloader** ROM state, `03e7:f63b` = the **app firmware** loaded. Seeing it *cycle repeatedly* means the device keeps **re-enumerating/resetting** (i.e. it's a *symptom* of the power/cable/watchdog/thermal faults above, not a separate bug). — https://github.com/luxonis/depthai/issues/518, https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/issues/560
- **Disconnect WITHOUT under-voltage flag:** consistent with **transient peak-current brown-out** (the spike is too brief to latch the Pi's under-voltage detector) and/or the **watchdog reset** path — both drop the link without a sustained under-voltage event.
- **Longer-but-not-immune on USB2:** matches the documented cable/power story — USB2 lowers bandwidth/power demand so the fault is rarer, but doesn't remove a power-headroom or thermal root cause.

### Version / firmware notes
- **depthai-ros:** older 2.10.x (issue #657) showed the long-run 100%-CPU/stall on Pi 5; the driver has had ongoing X_LINK/enumeration fragility through 2.26 (#560). No release is documented as a clean *fix* for power/thermal disconnects — those are hardware-class. Prefer a **recent depthai-ros / depthai-core** to get watchdog/timeout env-var support and enumeration fixes.
- **Bootloader update path:** the OAK bootloader is flashable via DepthAI — `depthai/utilities/device_manager.py` (GUI) or `dai.DeviceBootloader.flashBootloader(...)` in code; the **#560** device was on bootloader 0.028, so updating bootloader/firmware to current is the supported path. (Watchdog disable in bootloader had its own bug, #794.) — https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/794

## Which most likely match our case (ranked)

1. **Peak-current brown-out from the Pi-4's USB port (power headroom).** Best fit: disconnect *without* under-voltage (transient spike), USB2 "longer-but-not-immune," and the documented RPi4 1.2 A limit + OAK ~1 A + spikes. **Fix to try first: powered USB hub or external/Y-adapter power.** This is the single highest-probability root cause for our 2485↔f63b cycling.
2. **Marginal/long USB cable + USB3 instability.** Strongly supported and explains why USB2 helps but doesn't cure it. **Fix: short, quality USB3 cable (active if long); keep forced USB2 as mitigation.**
3. **MyriadX thermal auto-shutdown at 105 °C** under sustained stereo load on a fanless Pi-bound robot — presents exactly like a disconnect, and our depth-only continuous load keeps the VPU hot. **Fix: heatsink/airflow + reduce FPS/resolution/outputs.** (We already run 6 fps / 400p / no-NN, which lowers but doesn't eliminate this — worth a temperature check.)
4. **depthai-ros long-run 100%-CPU stall (issue #657).** Near-identical platform (Pi + TurtleBot 4 + depthai-ros). Matches our documented "OAK depth stalls, node alive at 0 Hz" gotcha — but that's a *stall*, not the *2485↔f63b re-enumeration cycling*, so it's a secondary suspect. **Fix: watchdog/relaunch (matches our SIGTERM-by-PID recovery).**
5. **Watchdog/enumeration timing races (#291/#420/#560).** Plausible contributor to failed *re*connects after a drop; tune `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY` / `DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT` and update bootloader, but unlikely the primary cause of repeated spontaneous cycling.

Net: order of attack — (1) powered hub / external power, (2) short quality cable + keep USB2, (3) verify VPU temp, (4) upgrade depthai-ros/bootloader and widen watchdog timeouts.

Sources:
- https://forums.raspberrypi.com/viewtopic.php?t=364587
- https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/deploy/to-rpi/
- https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/deploy/usb-deployment-guide
- https://docs.luxonis.com/software-v3/depthai/depthai-components/device/
- https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/environmental-specifications/operating-temperature
- https://discuss.luxonis.com/d/603-overheaterror-temperature-above-105c
- https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/issues/657
- https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/issues/560
- https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/420
- https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/291
